Companies are facing a crucial moment in AI adoption: while 42% of large companies have used AI, 40% are stuck in exploration and experimentation and cannot capture the operational benefits of their competitors.
The data show a growing gap between AI leaders and Laggards.
Organisations that already use AI double – 59% have accelerated investment over the last 24 months – suggesting measurable returns.
Financial services lead adoption with about half actively with AI, while telecommunications routes with 37%.
Geographical differences are strong: India (59%), the UAE (58%) and Singapore (53%) have an active deployment rate almost twice as high as Spain (28%), Australia (29%) and France (26%).
Primary use cases focus on operational efficiency – IT process automation (33%), security and threat detection (26%) and AI governance (25%) – and not on experimental applications.
Three specific obstacles are responsible for most blocked implementations: limited AI competence and expertise (33%), data complexity (25%) and ethical concerns (23%).
The skills gap is acute: one in five organisations lacks qualified staff to use AI tools, and 16% cannot find qualified staff.
Only 34% train existing employees to work with AI, even if 55% of AI-based companies use the technology specifically to reduce manual tasks.

A worrying lack of confidence leads to these challenges: while 85% of IT professionals believe that consumers prefer companies with transparent AI practices, less than half have implemented guarantees – only 27% actively reduce biases, 37% track data progress and 44% have developed ethical AI guidelines.
Cost reduction (42%) and ever more accessible instruments (45%) are driving adoption decisions forward, but 21% continue to call high prices a hindrance.

Exploration organisations should prioritise the closing of the skills gap through targeted reskilling programs, currently run by only one-third of companies, as labour shortages block more deployment opportunities than any other factor and the barrier is the most immediate within organisational control.
